content.problemStatement:
Chef is going to meet NNN people. Each person speaks one of the five languages: AAA, BBB, CCC, DDD, or EEE. Chef does not know any language at the start. To communicate, he has two options:


Chef can learn a language for a fixed cost CCC. Once he learns a language, he can talk with everyone who speaks that language.
Chef can hire a translator to help him. The cost of hiring a translator goes up each time he uses one, no matter which language it is. For example, his first use costs 111 unit, his second use costs 222 units, his third use costs 333 units, and so on. So if he has already used a translator three times, the next time will cost him 444 units.
You are given a string SSS of length NNN where SiS_iSi​ represents the language spoken by the ithi_{th}ith​ person. Your task is to find the minimum total cost for Chef to talk with all NNN people.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and CCC — the number of people and the cost to learn any language, respectively.
The second line contains a string SSS of length NNN where each character is one of AAA, BBB, CCC, DDD, or EEE. SiS_iSi​ is the language spoken by the ithi_{th}ith​ person.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum total cost for Chef to talk with all NNN people.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1: Chef needs to meet 333 people who all speak language AAA, and he can either learn language AAA for 101010 units or use a translator for each conversation with incremental costs of 111, 222, and 333 units respectively. Using translators, the total cost adds up to 666, which is lower than the fixed cost of 101010, so the minimum cost is 666.
Test Case 2: 333 people speaking AAA, BBB, and CCC and a learning cost of 222, if Chef uses translators for all three, the cost would be 1+2+3=61 + 2 + 3 = 61+2+3=6. However, if he learns one language (say AAA) for 222 units and then uses translators for the remaining two people (costing 111 and 222 units), the total comes to 2+3=52 + 3 = 52+3=5, which is optimal. It can be proven that the above strategy results in the minimum possible cost.
Test Case 3: 222 people speaking AAA and BBB with a learning cost of 333. Using translators for both results in a cost of 1+2=31 + 2 = 31+2=3, which is as good as any mixed strategy (for instance, learning one language and translating for the other would exceed 333), so the minimum cost is 333.
Test Case 4: Chef meets 555 people speaking DDD, CCC, BBB, AAA, and EEE with a learning cost of 444. If he relies solely on translators, the total cost would be 1+2+3+4+5=151 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 = 151+2+3+4+5=15. Alternatively, if he learns one language (for example, AAA) for 444 units and translates for the other four people with costs of 111, 222, 333, and 444, the total cost is 4+10=144 + 10 = 144+10=14. It can be proven that the above strategy results in the minimum possible cost.
